draft.introduction: Pliny the Younger’s letter to Emperor Trajan (c. 112 CE) is the earliest pagan account of Christian worship practices. As governor of Bithynia, Pliny asks how to handle accused Christians and describes their meetings, hymns, and communal meals.

translation.text: It is my custom, lord, to refer to you all matters on which I am in doubt. They declared that the sum of their guilt or error had been that they were accustomed to meet on a fixed day before dawn and sing responsively a hymn to Christ as to a god, and to bind themselves by oath not to commit any wickedness. After which it was their custom to depart and to assemble again to partake of food—ordinary and innocent food.
translation.original_text: Affirmabant autem hanc fuisse summam vel culpae suae vel erroris, quod essent soliti stato die ante lucem convenire carmenque Christo quasi deo dicere secum invicem.

translator_notes.note: The phrase carmenque Christo quasi deo dicere (“sing a hymn to Christ as to a god”) is the earliest pagan testimony to Christian worship of Christ as divine. Pliny’s matter-of-fact administrative tone makes this one of the most valuable documents for understanding how early Christianity appeared to Roman officials.
